messages.content:
Write a conclusion for this essay: "Texts possess the remarkable power of reflecting back to us that which we recognize in ourselves. We interact with them vicariously; seeing our own struggles, desires and aspirations in the characters we engage with. This humanising quality of literature allows us to examine our lives and emotions from a more objective perspective. Moreover, literature is the key to unlocking the countless parallel worlds that exist alongside our own, each brimming with its own unique perspectives and experiences. The idea that texts serve as mirrors through which we better understand ourselves and the world around us is particularly evident in Richard III by William Shakespeare and Looking for Richard by Al Pacino. In Looking for Richard, Pacino includes his rendition of the play, while also granting multiple views on themes of power, ambition and human nature in his other sections. By showing us Shakespeare’s original work alongside his interviews and discussions, we get to see Shakespeare’s own views of those aforementioned themes through the words, and Pacino’s view through the acting and directing of the scene. Shakespeare tells us his view through the content of his writing (what he is saying directly), the format of this writing (the manner in which he says it, including techniques such as metaphors) and the context of his writing (the time period he is writing in and the views and recent events occurring within it). Al Pacino tells us his views directly in conversation in Looking for Richard, his choices for the location to film the play, his choice of cast, and the way he acts when in the role of Richard. The notion of seeing oneself in the text as well as parallels between texts, is exemplified through these two works.

In Richard III, Shakespeare portrays the titular character’s intense ambition and lust for power, as he works his way to the throne by several unsavoury means. This is intentionally done by Shakespeare to mirror our own innate desire for success and recognition, with us often making sacrifices in order to achieve our goals. Like Richard, many compromise their own morals in the process as well.  This allows us to reflect on our own ambitions and thought processes, and gain a better understanding of our own desires and motivations as humans. Similarly, in Looking for Richard, Al Pacino explores Richard III as a character through interviews with scholars and actors, as well as reenactments of scenes from the play. In doing so, Pacino allows us to gain multiple new perspectives on the play, along with the reasons that those interviewed had those views. For example, one person interviewed, for example, was very passionate about Shakespeare, saying “When we speak with no feelings he get nothing out of our society. We should speak like Shakespeare. We should introduce Shakespeare into the academic.”, while another simply said “Yeah, it's boring.” upon being asked his opinion. This allows us to see how our own experiences can influence our interpretation of a text and open our minds to new concepts. This is exemplified through Pacino’s personal reflections and ideas, and how this affected his portrayal of Richard III. Pacino says in the film that “Nobody knows who Richard III is. Nobody!” This shines some light on the reason he decided to create the film in the first place; to inform people on Richard III, both as a play and a person. This is backed up by what he said shortly after: “I think the question is what is the understanding? The understanding is simply can you follow a storyline. We provided this docu-drama type thing to inform some of the scenes, so you know where you are.”  Ultimately, both Richard III and Looking for Richard serve as mirrors which reflect back important truths about ourselves and the world around us. Depending on the author and the time in which they live, the methods used to cause the audience to reflect on themselves may change. This shows that in all cases, the author is actively conscious of the fact that the work that they are writing is made for eyes other than their own, and thus must be presented in a way that is readily digestible by those consuming it to cause the desired effect. 

Both Richard III and Looking for Richard also depict the importance of language in shaping our perception of the world around us. In Shakespeare’s play, Richard, as a Machiavellian villain is expected to, frequently uses language as a tool to manipulate those around him, playing on their weaknesses and doubts to secure his position on the throne. Likewise, in Looking for Richard, Pacino highlights the power of language in shaping our understanding of the world. The very techniques and content of his speech is a method intentionally undertaken to manipulate the audience into sharing his beliefs, though not as maliciously as Richard. In this, we are able to see the creator in his creation and thus connect to them. This is of course just as applicable to Al Pacino in Looking for Richard as it is to Shakespeare in Richard III. We see Shakespeare in Richard III through the themes of deception, power struggle, and justice within the play, which all reflect the actual contemporary politics of his time. For example, Richard’s downfall is reflective of the belief of “Rota Fortuna”, or “the Wheel of Fortune” that was common in the Tudor era and included in other plays written by Shakespeare, such as in Hamlet: “slings and arrows of outrageous fortune". The concept holds that those who fortune favours consistently throughout their lives will inevitably face a fall from grace. Belief in Rota Fortuna was influenced by the content of the Bible to a degree, which played into the broader concept of divine justice. This is seen in Ecclesiastes 9:11, which says “I returned, and saw under the sun, that the race is not to the swift, nor the battle to the strong, neither yet bread to the wise, nor yet riches to men of understanding, nor yet favour to men of skill; but time and chance happeneth to them all.” In Act I, Scene III, Queen Margaret exclaims in an aside (regarding Richard) “Which God revenge!”. This foreshadows Richard’s predestined downfall as a consequence of him forsaking God through his actions. In all this, we see both Shakespeare’s personal and religious beliefs in the very words he has written."

In conclusion, texts have the power to reflect back important truths about ourselves and the world around us. This is exemplified through the humanising qualities of literature, which allow us to examine our lives and emotions from a more objective perspective. The idea of seeing oneself in the text as well as the parallels between texts is evident in Richard III by Shakespeare and Looking for Richard by Al Pacino. Both works depict the importance of language in shaping our perception of the world and the power dynamics within it. By analysing the themes and context of these works, we gain a better understanding of the authors and the influence they have on their creations. Overall, literature serves as a timeless mirror that reflects the complexity and beauty of the human experience, allowing us to learn, grow, and connect through shared experiences and perspectives.
Make that sound like it was written by me

In conclusion, I firmly believe that texts hold a remarkable power to reflect back to us that which we recognize in ourselves. Through literature, we are able to vicariously engage with characters and their struggles, desires, and aspirations, which allows us to examine our own lives and emotions in a more objective perspective. In my opinion, the idea of seeing ourselves in the text, as well as parallels between texts, is particularly evident in Richard III by William Shakespeare and Looking for Richard by Al Pacino. I am of the firm belief that both works highlight the importance of language in shaping our perception of the world around us and the power dynamics within it. By analysing the themes and context of these works, I have come to gain a better understanding of the authors and the inspiration that went into their creations. In summary, I hold literature in high regard as it serves as a timeless mirror that reflects the complexity and beauty of the human experience, allowing us to learn, grow, and connect through shared experiences and perspectives.
Make it match the sound of the rest of the essay

In retrospect, it is unequivocal to me that texts possess an incredible power to reflect back to us that which we recognize in ourselves. Through literature, we are able to see our own struggles, desires, and aspirations through the characters we engage with. This allows us to examine our lives and emotions from a more objective perspective, gaining a deeper understanding of ourselves and the world around us. The notion of seeing oneself in the text, as well as the parallels between texts, finds particular resonance in William Shakespeare's Richard III and Al Pacino's Looking for Richard. Both works offer poignant insights into the importance of language in shaping our perceptions of power dynamics in society. By closely analyzing and deconstructing these works, I have grown to appreciate the authors and the inspiration that drove them to create such timeless pieces of literature. Ultimately, literature serves as an enduring mirror that captures the complexity and beauty of the human experience, allowing us to learn, grow, and connect through shared experiences and perspectives.
Make it not first person

In conclusion, it is clear that texts possess an exceptional power to reflect back to readers that which they recognize in themselves. Through literature, characters and their struggles, desires, and aspirations allow readers to examine their own lives and emotions from a more objective perspective. The notion of seeing oneself in the text, as well as parallels between texts, is particularly evident in Richard III by William Shakespeare and Looking for Richard by Al Pacino, both of which offer valuable insights into the power dynamics of society and the importance of language in shaping our perceptions. By analyzing these works closely, readers can gain a deeper appreciation of the authors and the inspiration that drove them to create such timeless pieces of literature. Ultimately, literature serves as an enduring mirror that captures the complexity and beauty of the human experience, allowing us to learn, grow, and connect through shared experiences and perspectives.
Make it match the language and sound of the rest of the essay

In conclusion, it is evident that texts hold an incredible power to reflect back to readers that which is recognized in themselves. Through literature, characters with whom readers can vicariously interact allow for reflective introspection, offering insight into their struggles, desires, and aspirations and enabling us to explore our own lives and emotions objectively. The concept of seeing oneself in the text, as well as detecting parallels between texts, is primarily explored in William Shakespeare's Richard III and Al Pacino's Looking for Richard, both of which present compelling observations concerning the linguistic and social dynamics at play in society. By carefully examining these works, readers are privy to the inspiration driving the authors and their creative efforts. Ultimately, literature serves as a mirror that reflects the intricacies and beauty of the human experience, providing a communal platform for growth, learning, and understanding through shared experiences and perspectives.
Make it more unique

In closing, it is clear that written works have the remarkable capacity to reflect our innermost selves. Through literature, we see our struggles, aspirations, and desires embodied in the characters we experience vicariously. This distinctive feature of literature compels us to examine our lives through an objective lens, fostering deeper insight into our emotions and experiences. However, the idea of seeing oneself in the text, along with identifying parallelisms between works, finds exceptional resonance in Richard III by Shakespeare and Looking for Richard by Pacino. Both works are thought-provoking, as they delve into the intricate complexities of power dynamics in society and the role of language in shaping perception. By contextualizing these works and situating them in their respective eras, readers can gain a nuanced appreciation of the authors' creativity, which produced enduringly relevant pieces of literature that have stood the test of time. Ultimately, literature creates a replicate of our individual and shared experiences, promoting growth, learning, and awareness through the exploration of divergent perspectives.

relate it more to this statement without quoting it "Texts are a mirror through which we often see both ourselves and other texts. This process
allows us to come to a better understanding and appreciation of both texts."

The sentiment of seeing both oneself and other texts in written works resonates strongly with the idea that literature is a mirror reflecting the beauty and intricacy of the human experience. By immersing oneself in different texts, one can recognize our collective struggles, desire, and aspirations in the characters that we engage with. This leads to introspective reflection, allowing individuals to gain a better understanding of their emotions, experiences, and perspectives from an objective viewpoint. Additionally, exploring multiple works opens up opportunities to identify themes, techniques, and motifs across different texts, leading to a deeper appreciation and understanding of the craft of writing. Through this process of engaging with texts, readers can gain a more nuanced understanding of themselves and their place in the world. Ultimately, written works serve as a bridge that connects the author and reader, forging an unbreakable sense of community founded on a shared love and appreciation of literature.
whats a good synonym for better
An alternative for "better" could be improved, enhanced, superior, more proficient, or elevated.
in the context of "engaging with the texts they read better"
In the context of "engaging with the texts they read better," some synonyms could be engaging with the texts they read more effectively, more competently, more skillfully, or more proficiently.
how would i say "texts that people read" when the texts involve films

When referring to films as texts that people read, one could use the phrase "visual texts" or "cinematic texts" instead of simply "texts." This distinguishes them from traditional written texts while still conveying the idea that they are a type of literary work. For example, "visual texts such as films" or "cinematic texts that people watch."

Make this sound better: "Ultimately, written works serve as a bridge that connects the author and reader, forging an unbreakable sense of community founded on a shared love and appreciation of literature."

In the end, literary works assume the role of a bridge that links the author to the reader, cultivating an unbreakable sense of camaraderie that extends from a mutual admiration and affection for the art of writing. It is this unique connection, underpinned by a shared passion for literature, that lies at the heart of the literary experience, transcending boundaries and bringing people together in ways that only the written word can.
